questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Neon occurs naturally in three isotopes with the abundance of each isotope shown in the table below:
IsotopeAbundance (%)
20Ne90.48
21Ne0.27
22Ne9.25
questions.answer: If the candidate omitted the “+” sign for the positive numbers, and the numerical answers are correct, deduct 1 mark only.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: What is meant by the term ‘isotope’?
sub_questions.answer: Atoms with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. / Atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers. (Accept giving the answer with a specific example. E.g. All neon atoms have 10 protons, but they can have 10, 11 or 12 neutrons. NOT Accept ions, elements and molecules.）

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Calculate the relative atomic mass of neon.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Give one daily application of neon.
sub_questions.answer: Gas for filling luminous advertisement tubes / neon tubes / neon signs / neon lights (NOT Accept fluorescent tubes)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why the boiling point of neon is lower than that of oxygen.
sub_questions.answer:
Neon is monoatomic whereas oxygen is diatomic.  \( O_{2} \)  molecule has larger molecular size than Ne molecule. (NOT Accept larger molecular mass).
Thus stronger van der Waals' force / stronger intermolecular force among  \( O_{2} \)  molecules. (NOT Accept VDW force)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text:
Poly(ethenyl ethanoate) is a polymer. Its monomer is ethenyl ethanoate with the structure shown below:

[IMG:0.jpg]
questions.answer:
If the units given in 8(c)(i), 8(c)(ii) and 8(d)(i) are wrong, but they are the same, deduct 1 mark only. If those incorrect units are not the same in each questions, marks are deducted individually.
* step mark
†: correct spelling

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Ethene is the raw material used in making ethenyl ethanoate. Ethene can be produced from hydrocarbons of higher molecular mass by an important industrial process.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain why this process is important.
children.answer:
This process can produce small molecules / alkenes / ethene / petrol / hydrocarbons of lower molecular masses from large hydrocarbons to meet the industrial demand / to make useful materials / to make useful fuels. /
This process can produce more small molecules / alkenes / ethene / petrol / hydrocarbons of lower molecular masses from large hydrocarbons.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the structure of poly(ethenyl ethanoate).
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:ans_q2_ii_0.jpg]

● CH₂ and OC(=O)CH₃ can be written in the condensed form.
● The brackets and “n” SHOULD NOT be omitted.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Ethyl ethanoate is an organic solvent.
sub_questions.answer:
(i)
CH₃CH₂—O—C—CH₃
● The ester group should not be written in the condensed form. CH₃COOCH₂CH₃ is NOT acceptable.
● The bond angle of the ester group NOT necessary be drawn as 120°

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw the structure of ethyl ethanoate.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest a chemical test to show how to distinguish between ethenyl ethanoate and ethyl ethanoate.
children.answer:
Bromine test – ethenyl ethanoate can decolourise orange / brown / yellow bromine / Br₂ solution immediately while ethyl ethanoate cannot. (NOT Accept Br). (Require to mention the reaction of  \( Br_{2} \)  with ethenyl ethanoate is much faster than ethyl ethanoate) /
Treating with acidified potassium permanganate solution - ethenyl ethanoate can decolourise purple acidified potassium permanganate solution while ethyl ethanoate cannot. (Also accept treating with potassium permanganate solution (without acidification) with the correct descriptions of observations – change from purple to brown (ppt)).
†: correct spelling

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
Consider the information concerning the lemon cells shown in the diagrams below :

[IMG:0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: What is the function of the lemons in these cells?
sub_questions.answer: Provide  \( H^{+} \) /ions / electrolyte for the chemical cell.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
By completing the table below, arrange metal X, metal Y and copper in increasing order of reducing power.

[IMG:1.jpg]
sub_questions.answer: Copper, Metal Y, Metal X / Cu, Y, X

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: For Cell 1, write the half equation for the change that occurs at :

children.label: i
children.question_text: metal X strip (X is a group II metal), and

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: For Cell 2, would the metal Y strip be the positive electrode if the copper strip is replaced with a silver strip? Explain your answer.
sub_questions.answer: No, the metal Y strip would be the negative electrode. It is because silver is lower than copper in the electrochemical series / silver is less reactive than copper. So silver should be lower than Y in the electrochemical series / less reactive than Y.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: With the aid of a diagram, explain the formation of hydrogen bonding in hydrogen fluoride.
questions.answer:
[IMG:ans_q4_0.jpg]

● The drawing should show at least TWO HF molecules.
● Should show complete HF molecules.
● Should indicate the hydrogen bonds by dashed lines.
● Each H atom can only form one hydrogen bond.
● F-H-F angle not necessary be drawn as  \( 180^{\circ} \)  in the drawing.
Fluorine / F is a highly very electronegative element. 
The H-F covalent bond is very /highly polar / The H-F molecule is highly polarized.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: In order to prepare 50 dm \( ^{3} \)  of 0.1 M  \( CuSO_{4(aq)} \) , an inexperienced electroplating worker added the required exact amount of  \( CuSO_{4} \cdot 5H_{2}O(s) \)  to water in a plastic container. He then stirred the mixture with an iron rod until the  \( CuSO_{4} \cdot 5H_{2}O(s) \) , dissolved completely. Finally, he sent a sample of the solution to the Quality Control Laboratory for analysis, but found that the concentration of  \( CuSO_{4(aq)} \)  was lower than 0.1 M.
questions.answer:
(c)

Correct overall diagram – 1 mark
Correct labels (copper, object (accept omitting the object),  \( \mathrm{CuSO_{4}(aq)/Cu^{2+}(aq)} \) ) - 1 mark
Correct direction of the battery / power supply – 1 mark
● Label not accept  \( CuSO_{4}\cdot5H_{2}O \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: With the aid of a chemical equation, explain why the concentration of the  \( CuSO_{4}(aq) \)  prepared was lower than 0.1 M.
sub_questions.answer:
Displacement reaction occurred when the iron rod is dipped into the copper(II) sulphate solution. / Some copper(II) ions ( \( Cu^{2+} \) ) are reduced and deposited onto the surface of the iron rod as copper metal.
\( Cu^{2+}(aq) + Fe(s) \rightarrow Cu(s) + Fe^{2+}(aq) / CuSO_{4}(aq) + Fe(s) \rightarrow Cu(s) + FeSO_{4}(aq) \)  (Not accept CuSO_{4}.5H_{2}O; physical states are not required.)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: The worker used the prepared  \( CuSO_{4}(aq) \)  to coat a layer of copper on a metallic object by electrolysis. He used an unreasonably high voltage, and found that some bubbles were formed on the object and the copper layer easily flaked off.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain why copper can be coated on the metallic object by electrolysis.
children.answer: Copper is lower than hydrogen in the electrochemical series /  \( Cu^{2+} \)  is discharged preferentially than  \( H^{+} \)  when a current is applied.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest what the bubbles were, and explain why the copper layer easily flaked off.
children.answer:
Hydrogen gas /  \( H_{2} \)
The hydrogen gas bubbles hinder the deposition of copper on the surface of the metallic object, hence causing the copper metal deposited easily flaked off.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of the experimental set-up used in a laboratory for coating a layer of copper on a metallic object by electrolysis.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:ans_q5_0.jpg]

Correct overall diagram - 1 mark
Correct labels (copper, object (accept omitting the object), \(CuSO_{4}(aq)\) / \(Cu^{2+}(aq)\) ) - 1 mark
Correct direction of the battery / power supply - 1 mark
• Label not accept \(CuSO_{4}•5H_{2}O\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Outline the steps in preparing solid lead(II) sulphate from solid lead(II) nitrate. You have to state the additional chemical reagents that are required, but need NOT mention the apparatus involved.
questions.answer:
• Dissolve solid lead(II) nitrate in water,
• Then mix with (excess) sulphuric acid /  \( K_{2}SO_{4} \)  /  \( Na_{2}SO_{4} \)  solution. (Also accept other examples of soluble sulphates) (Remark: if the candidate considered  \( PbSO_{4} \)  as soluble salt – 0 mark for this part).
• Filter the mixture to obtain the residue (PbSO_{4}), wash it with deionised water and then dry in oven.
• Communication Mark
(Chemical knowledge = 0 or 1: no communication mark
Chemical knowledge = 2 or 3: for easily understandable complete sentences, communication mark = 1, otherwise = 0)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
A fertiliser only contains ammonium nitrate ( \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \) ) and potassium chloride (KCl). An experiment was performed to determine the percentage by mass of  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  in this fertiliser. The set-up used is shown below:

[IMG:0.jpg]

The KOH(aq) was added slowly to the fertiliser and the mixture formed was heated gently. The ammonia liberated from the reaction between  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  and KOH was first cooled in a condenser, and then passed through an inverted funnel to a solution containing 0.0485 mol of HCl. The solution was finally made up to 100.00 cm \( ^{3} \)  and labelled 'S'.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write an ionic equation for the reaction between  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  and KOH.
sub_questions.answer:
\( NH_{4}^{+} + OH^{-} \rightarrow NH_{3} + H_{2}O \)  (Neglect physical state) 
(NOT Accept:  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} + OH^{-} \rightarrow NH_{3} + NO_{3}^{-} + H_{2}O \) )

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest the potential hazard of one of the chemicals used.
sub_questions.answer:
The KOH is (very) corrosive. /  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  is explosive /  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  is flammable / HCl is corrosive.
(Should specify the chemical; NOT Accept harmful / toxic / poisonous; NOT Accept HCl Fume is corrosive as dilute HCl is involved.)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Given that ammonia is very soluble in water, state the advantage of using an inverted funnel.
sub_questions.answer: Prevent sucking back as  \( \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  is very soluble. (Accept prevent HCl sucking upwards or similar descriptions) / Increase the surface area for dissolving  \( \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: 25.00 cm \( ^{3} \)  of ‘S’ was transferred to a conical flask, and then titrated with 0.100M NaOH(aq) using methyl orange as an indicator. 41.00 cm \( ^{3} \)  of the NaOH(aq) was required to reach the end point.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Name the apparatus that should be used to transfer  \( 25.00 \, cm^{3} \)  of 'S'.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: State the colour change at the end point of the titration.
children.answer: Changes from red to orange. (NOT Accept red to yellow).

children.label: iii
children.question_text:
Calculate the percentage by mass of  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  in this fertiliser. 
(Molar mass of  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} = 80.0 \, g \) )
children.answer:
Number of moles of HCl(aq) remained in the beaker :
\(  = 0.100 \times 0.04100 \times (100.00 \div 25.00) = 0.0164  \)
Number of moles of  \( \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \)  produced
\(  = 0.0485 - 0.0164 = 0.0321  \)
Percentage by mass of  \( NH_{4}NO_{3} \)  in the fertiliser
\(  = (0.0321 \times 80) / 3.150 \times 100\% = 81.5\% / 81.5  \)
(Accept 81.52% / 82.5% / 82.54%)

sub_questions.label: e
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest a test to show the presence of a potassium-containing compound in the fertiliser.
sub_questions.answer: Flame test – gives a lilac / purple / light purple flame. (Not Accept deep purple and violet)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Potassium hydrogencarbonate ( \( KHCO_{3} \) ) can be used to bake bread. Upon heating,  \( KHCO_{3} \)  decomposes into  \( K_{2}CO_{3} \) ,  \( H_{2}O \)  and  \( CO_{2} \) .

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Explain the purpose of using  \( KHCO_{3} \)  in bread baking.
sub_questions.answer: \( CO_{2} \)  gas produced makes the bread rise / spongy / soft.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Write the chemical equation for the decomposition of  \( KHCO_{3} \)  upon heating.
sub_questions.answer: \( 2\ KHCO_{3} \rightarrow K_{2}CO_{3} + H_{2}O + CO_{2} / KHCO_{3} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} K_{2}CO_{3} + \frac{1}{2} H_{2}O + \frac{1}{2} CO_{2} \)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
The enthalpy change of decomposition of \( KHCO_{3}(s) \) can be determined indirectly from the enthalpy changes of the following two reactions : \( KHCO_{3}(s)+HCl(aq)\rightarrow KCl(aq)+H_{2}O(l)+CO_{2}(g)\quad\cdots\cdots\cdots Reaction (1)\) \( K_{2}CO_{3}(s)+2HCl(aq)\rightarrow2KCl(aq)+H_{2}O(l)+CO_{2}(g)\quad\cdots\cdots\cdots Reaction (2) \) \(\) In an experiment to determine the enthalpy change of Reaction (1), 3.39 g of \( KHCO_{3}(s) \) was added to excess HCl(aq) in an expanded polystyrene cup. The experimental data obtained are shown below:
Initial temperature of the reacting solution:25.8 \( {}^{\circ} \) C
Final temperature of the reacting solution:20.2 \( {}^{\circ} \) C
Mass of the resulting solution:27.5 g
Specific heat capacity of the contents:4.3 J g \( ^{-1} \) K \( ^{-1} \)
Molar mass of KHCO \( _{3} \) :100.1 g

children.label: i
children.question_text: Assuming that the heat capacity of the cup used is negligible, calculate the enthalpy change of Reaction (1) from the above data.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: In another experiment performed under the same condition, the enthalpy change of Reaction (2) was found to be \( -49.1\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\). Calculate the enthalpy change of decomposition of \(KHCO_{3}(s)\) under the experimental conditions.
children.answer:
\( \Delta H = 19.6-(-49.1 \times \frac{1}{2}((+19.6 \times 2)-(-49.1))\)
\( = +44.15\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \) (Accept +44.1 to +44.2) (the unit must be correct)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
According to the literature, the standard enthalpy changes of formation of \( K_{2}CO_{3}(s) \) , \( KHCO_{3}(s) \) , \( CO_{2}(g) \) and \( H_{2}O(l) \) are as follows :
Compound\( \Delta H_{f}^{\circ} \) , \( ^{298}/kJ mol^{-1} \)
\( K_{2}CO_{3}(s) \)-1146
\( KHCO_{3}(s) \)-959
\( CO_{2}(g) \)-394
\( H_{2}O(l) \)-286

children.label: i
children.question_text: Using the given information, calculate the standard enthalpy change of decomposition of  \( KHCO_{3}(s) \) .
children.answer: \( \Delta H = \frac{1}{2}(-1146 - 394 - 286 - (-959 \times 2)) = +46 \, kJ \, mol^{-1} \)  (the unit must be correct)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest why the answers obtained from (c)(ii) and (d)(i) are different.
children.answer: Not performing the experiment in standard conditions. / Heat transfer with the surroundings. / The heat capacity of the container was neglected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows an experimental set-up for investigating the factors affecting rusting.

[IMG:0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: What would be observed if an iron nail in the above set-up rusts?
sub_questions.answer: Blue / Blue and pink colouration would be observed near the iron nail which rusts. (Accept green to blue / yellow to blue)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest which of the iron nails in the above set-up would NOT rust during the experiment. Explain your answer.
sub_questions.answer:
Both iron nail B and iron nail C would not rust.
For iron nail B, as Mg is higher than iron in the metal reactivity series (with further explanation such as: the magnesium ribbon loses electrons more readily and will become  \( Mg^{2+} \)  / Mg corrodes more readily). /
For iron nail B, the magnesium ribbon protects the iron nail from rusting by sacrificial protection.
For iron nail C, as it is sealed with grease, the iron cannot contact with water and for air (oxygen), so rusting cannot occur.

● Give two correct explanations without referring to the corresponding iron nails – 1 mark

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: Suggest THREE measures for reducing the emission of air pollutants upon using fossil fuels.
questions.answer:
(Any three)
• Install catalytic converters in car
• Use unleaded petrol
• Replace diesel with LPG for vehicles / Use LPG for vehicles/mini-bus/bus/taxi
• Install scrubbers in power plant
• Using Ultra Low Sulphur Diesel / Use low sulphur coal in power plant / use low sulphur fuels.
• Use electrostatic precipitatior
• Remove dust by mechanical filtering

(NOT accept use less fossil fuels / use alternative fuels)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
In an experiment,  \( 50 \, cm^{3} \)  of  \( 2.0 \, M \, HCl(aq) \)  was added to a conical flask containing  \( 2.0 \, g \)  of zinc powder. The curve in the graph below shows the volume, measured at room temperature and pressure, of the hydrogen gas liberated in the first few minutes of the experiment. The dotted line in the graph is the tangent to the curve at the start of the reaction.

[IMG:0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: The ‘initial rate’ of a reaction is defined as the instantaneous rate at the start of the reaction. With reference to the graph above, calculate the initial rate of the reaction with respect to the volume of hydrogen gas liberated.
sub_questions.answer: Initial rate = 60/4 = 15 cm³/min (0.25 cm³/s) (Accept 14.8 - 15.2)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain qualitatively the effect on the initial rate of the reaction of replacing the 2.0 M HCl(aq) with 2.0 M H₂SO₄(aq).
sub_questions.answer:
HCl is a monobasic acid, while  \( H_{2}SO_{4} \)  is a dibasic acid. Initial rate increases if  \( H_{2}SO_{4} \)  is used. / Initial rate increases as the concentration of  \( H^{+} \) increases in 2.0 M  \( H_{2}SO_{4} \) . 
Therefore, the frequency of effective collisions increases.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.answer:
No. of mol of Zn = 2/65.4 = 0.031 mol
Vol of H₂ formed = 0.031 × 24000
= 734 cm³ / 0.734 dm³ (Accept 730-744 cm³ / 0.73-0.74 dm³)
(the unit must be correct)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Cinnamon, which can be used as a flavouring, contains cinnamaldehyde ( \( C_{9}H_{8}O \) ). The structure of cinnamaldehyde is shown below :

[IMG:0.jpg]
questions.answer:
(d)

(Accept the aldehyde group (CHO) is NOT reduced by  \( H_{2} \) /catalyst to give  \( CH_{2}OH \) )

(Accept other correct answers.)
If the candidate gave more than three steps, only the first three steps will be marked.
If a reaction/an intermediate is incorrect, it will not affect the marking of the subsequent steps as long as the chemistry of the subsequent steps is correct.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the trans-isomer for the above structure.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:Screenshot 2026-06-16 132555_2.jpg]

• The bond angles of the alkene should be about \(120 \degree\). The bonds drawn in \(90\degree\) are not accepted.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why ethyl ethanoate is a better solvent than water for dissolving cinnamaldehyde.
sub_questions.answer: Cinnamaldehyde is a non-polar compound which can dissolve in a relatively non-polar organic solvent like ethyl ethanoate. However, water is a polar solvent. / Both cinnamaldehyde and ethyl ethanoate are relatively non-polar compounds. Their molecules are attracted by weak intermolecular forces. / weak dipole-dipole interactions / van der Waals' forces.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
In an experiment to extract cinnamaldehyde from cinnamon, a solution containing only ethyl ethanoate and cinnamaldehyde is obtained after a series of steps. In order to separate these two compounds, simple distillation can be carried out. Draw a diagram for the set-up involved, and label the name of the distillate collected.

(Boiling points: cinnamaldehyde = 248 °C, ethyl ethanoate = 77 °C)
sub_questions.answer:

● No mark will be given to the drawing if:

The setup is a closed system
The top of the distillation head is open to air
A fractional column is included in the drawing
● 1 mark is given to the correct labeling and spelling of the distillate (ethyl ethanoate)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
Outline a synthetic route, with no more than three steps, to accomplish the following conversion. For each step, give the reagent(s), reaction conditions (as appropriate) and structure of the organic product.

[IMG:1.jpg]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Consider the reaction represented by the equation below :
\( \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{SCN}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\rightleftharpoons\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \)
In an experiment,  \( 25.0 \, cm^{3} \)  of  \( 0.010 \, M \, Fe_{2}(SO_{4})_{3} \)  (aq) and  \( 25.0 \, cm^{3} \)  of  \( 0.01 \, M \, KSCN \)  (aq) were mixed in a conical flask at room temperature, and equilibrium was attained.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: The concentration of  \( \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  in the mixture was 0.0043 M when equilibrium was attained. Calculate the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the above reaction at room temperature.
sub_questions.answer:
\( [Fe^{3+}(aq)]_{initial after mixing} = 0.010 \times 2 \times 1/2 = 0.01 M \)
\( \mathrm{[SCN^{-}(aq)]_{initial after mixing}=0.010\times1/2=0.005M} \)
\( \mathrm{Fe^{3+}(aq) + SCN^{-}(aq)\rightleftharpoons Fe(SCN)^{2+}(aq)} \)
Initial 0.01 | 0.005
Reacted 0.01 - 0.0043 | 0.005 - 0.0043
At eqm 0.0057 | 0.0007 | 0.0043
\( K_{c}=[Fe(SCN)^{2+}(aq)]/[Fe^{3+}(aq)][SCN^{-}(aq)] \)
\( =(0.0043)/0.0057\times0.0007 \)
\( 1078\ mol^{-1}\ dm^{3}\) (accept 1080, no mark for wrong unit)

For the  \( 2^{nd} \)  step mark:
Correct expression for  \( K_{c}-1 \)  mark
No expression is written, the numbers in "(0.0043) / 0.0057×0.0007" is correct – 1 mark
No expression, the numbers in "(0.0043) / 0.0057×0.007" is incorrect – 0 mark

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: It is known that  \( \mathrm{FePO}_{4}(\mathrm{s}) \)  is insoluble in water. Suggest what would be the effect on the equilibrium position if  \( \mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{PO}_{4}(\mathrm{s}) \)  is added to the equilibrium mixture.
sub_questions.answer: The equilibrium position will shift to the left hand side / reactant side.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows the conversion of an oil molecule X to a fat molecule Y.

[IMG:0.jpg]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Given that all alkyl groups in both X and Y are straight chains, label the chiral carbon(s) by using ‘ \( ^{*} \) ’ in the above diagram.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: With reference to (i), explain whether a change in optical activity is involved in the above conversion.
children.answer:
Yes. X has one chiral carbon and hence optically active, while Y does not have chiral carbons and hence optically inactive. Thus, there is a change in optical activity for the conversion.
\( ^{*} \) : step mark

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: One of the products in the alkaline hydrolysis of Y has a cleansing property. Explain the cleansing property of this product.
sub_questions.answer:
The  \( C_{17}H_{35}COO^{-} \)  ion has an ionic head (COO \( ^{-} \) ) and a hydrocarbon tail ( \( C_{17}H_{35} \) ). 
The hydrocarbon tail dissolves in grease droplets / is hydrophobic while the ionic head dissolves in water / is hydrophilic. 
The ionic heads of the grease droplets repel from each other and the dirts inside these droplets are then removed.
Communication Mark
(Chemical knowledge = 0 or 1: no communication mark
Chemical knowledge = 2 or 3: for easily understandable complete sentences, communication mark = 1, otherwise = 0)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: Use electron diagrams to illustrate, step by step, how  \( CH_{4} \)  reacts with  \( Br_{2} \)  under sunlight to form  \( CH_{3}Br \) . (Show electrons in the outermost shells only.)
questions.answer:
[IMG:ans_q14_b_0.jpg]

All four rows indicating (a), (b), (c) and (d) respectively.
(a), (b), (c) = 1+1+1
(a), (b), (d) = 1+1+1

● No marks are given if the numbers of electrons in the outermost shells are wrong.
● If more than three equations are written, marks are given to the correct ones (Max = 3).
● If the candidate used the wrong halogen atoms (F, Cl, I) in the drawing, deduct 1 mark).

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 12: Patterns in the Chemical World
questions.question_text:
Consider the following oxides :
Na₂O MgO Al₂O₃ SiO₂ P₄O₁₀ SO₂ Cl₂O

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Which of the oxides listed above can conduct electricity in molten state?
sub_questions.answer:
\( Na_{2}O \) ,  \( MgO \) ,  \( Al_{2}O_{3} \)
(Order is not important. 0 mark if more/less than these 3 oxides are listed.)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why  \( SiO_{2} \)  has the highest melting point among the covalent oxides listed above.
sub_questions.answer:
\( SiO_{2} \)  has a giant covalent structure, and the Si and O atoms are linked by strong covalent bonds. (Not accept strong covalent structure / giant covalent bonds)
Other covalent oxides are discrete molecules attracted by weak van der Waals' forces / weak intermolecular forces / weak dipolar interactions. (NOT accept VDW forces)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the reaction between  \( Al_{2}O_{3}(s) \)  and NaOH(aq).
sub_questions.answer:
\( Al_{2}O_{3} + 2OH^{-} + 3H_{2}O \rightarrow 2Al(OH)_{4}^{-} \)
or  \( Al_{2}O_{3} + 2NaOH + 3H_{2}O \rightarrow 2NaAl(OH)_{4} \)
